-66.0%

FCF Yield Per Share - TTM

8.28%

5.33%

(Source: Seeking Alpha)

The table shows that Grid is being discounted by the market on a forward EV/Sales multiple basis, likely due to its lower GAAP net income margin.

While GDYN may be attractively valued on a growth and balance sheet strength basis, its earnings quality is lower, and SBC is high.

For that to change, the company would need to either return to faster revenue growth, leading to bottom-line earnings acceleration, or reduce costs and SBC to improve EPS on a slower growth trajectory.

In summary, my outlook on GDYN remains a Hold due to:

- Strong balance sheet & secular tailwinds.

- GenAI monetization lag + growth deceleration (~12%).

- Weak earnings quality - low margins, high SBC, deteriorating operating leverage.

data.title: Greenwich LifeSciences: 'Buy' On Recent Open-Label Data And Patent Claim
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Greenwich LifeSciences (GLSI) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Greenwich LifeSciences: Halting Of Breast Cancer Recurrence With GLSI-100." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was in the process of advancing its phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study using GLSI-100 for the treatment of HER2-positive breast cancer patients who had residual disease or high-risk pathologic complete response [PCR] at surgery and who had completed both neoadjuvant and postoperative adjuvant trastuzumab-based regimens. The company is still in the process of running this phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study using this immunotherapy candidate to treat these HER2-positive breast cancer patients. However, the reason why I believe it is important to go over this company again is because it had two major updates relating to the development of this specific program.

The first of which is that it released open-label data from the phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study, which showed that non-HLA-A*02 HER2-positive breast cancer patients had an 80% reduction in recurrence rate. One item to mention here is that this open-label study data was achieved while keeping this study phase 3 study blind. Secondly, there were protocol changes with the FDA that allowed the company to be able to go after both HLA-A*02 and non-HLA-A*02 patients as part of this late-stage trial. This brings me to the next positive update from this program, which is that the company was able to file new patent claims based on this statistically significant open-label immune response and recurrence data.

Should the patent claims be upheld in court, then the company believes that it could be able to have patent protection of GLSI-100 through 2045. The key item here is that it would be able to target HER2-positive breast cancer patients regardless of HLA type [that is, to also go after non-HLA-A*02 patients]. With the key phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study using GLSI-100 targeting patients with HER2-positive breast cancer continuing, plus the release of open-label data from it and patent claim protection moving this program forward, I believe that this stock should remain as a "Buy."

Two New Key Developments For FLAMINGO-01 To Move Program Forward In A Good Way

As I mentioned above, Greenwich LifeSciences is continuing to advance its ongoing phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study for the treatment of patients with HER2-positive breast cancer. Before going over the key developments that occurred with this program, plus the newly released open-label data from FLAMINGO-01, I believe that it is important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being. Breast cancer [BC], as the name suggests, is the growth of malignant cells that form a tumor on the breast of a patient. In essence, DNA changes of cells cause them to grow uncontrollably, forming such a tumor. Such breast cancer cells begin in the milk ducts or milk-producing lobules of the breast. In the early stages, known as in situ, it is not deadly and can be treated. There is an ability for this cancer to spread to nearby breast tissue, and then that's when lumps [tumors] start to form. Furthermore, the real danger starts when the cancer spreads to other organs and/or the lymph nodes, known as metastases. The global breast cancer market size is projected to reach $40.11 billion by 2035. This is a very large market, but it is important to keep in mind that this company is focused on going after HER2-positive BC patients. This is not bad at all, because it is said that up to about 25% of all BCs are of HER2 status. What does this HER2 mean? In essence, a mutation of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 gene causes an excess amount of HER2 protein, and this fuels the BC growth. There are HER2-targeted therapies in place to treat these HER2-positive breast cancer patients, but despite this, they still have a high rate of disease recurrence. With that being said, the hope is that GLSI-100 is going to be enough to reduce recurrence for these patients. That is, to improve upon the 50% recurrence reduction that Herceptin and Kadcyla can achieve.

In order to determine if GLSI-100 [GP2 + GM-CSF] would be enough to prevent recurrence in these HER2-positive breast cancer patients, it is currently in the process of running the phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 trial. It is important to state that this company has set a conservative number of a 70% reduction in breast cancer recurrence. This is not bad, considering that the prior phase 2b study was noted to have achieved an 80% reduction in disease recurrence. The FLAMINGO-01 trial continues on, and the hope is that the company is going to be able to replicate what it achieved from the phase 2b study. Greenwich took a different strategic direction, which is that it changed the protocols to go after both HLA-A*02 and non-HLA-A*02 patients. With that being said, though, it can combine the analysis of both of these patient groups together and then evaluate the efficacy endpoint with increased statistical power. The point is that by doing this it could double the market that is possible for GP2 by up to $10 billion in revenue per year. The ability to go after such HER2-positive BC patients regardless of HLA type is the key for it to open the door of targeting a larger market opportunity.

This was made possible thanks to the two new key developments that happened for this company. The first of which is that it was able to release open-label study data from its phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study and still keep it going in a blinded manner. Specifically, it released preliminary data showcasing that GLSI-100 given to non-HLA-A*02 achieved an 80% reduction in recurrence rate. With this data on hand, the company also provided an update that it could file new patent claims based on this open-label immune response and recurrence data. This is not guaranteed, though, as the company has to obtain patent protection for this first in court. However, if successful, this would provide patent protection for GLSI-100 for targeting non-HLA-A*02 patients through 2045. Another item to mention about the patent claims is that the non-HLA-A*02 arm has a favorable comparison to the blinded HLA-A*02 arm. However, it is key to note that this trial remains blinded, and there is no assurance that the final outcome will turn out to be the same as what has been shown in the open-label immune response and recurrence data.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Greenwich LifeSciences had cash of $3,806,978 as of September 30, 2025. However, the company has not yet filed its 10-K SEC filing, and filed a document with the SEC that it would be late with this filing. Specifically, it would file such a 10-K report by April 15, 2026 or within the 15-days permitted under SEC rules. The mention for the late filing would be what is still under review according to this SEC Filing, which is that R&D expenses are anticipated to have increased by $4.6 million [35%]. Thus, bringing the expected R&D expense number to $17.5 million for the year ending December 31, 2025. The point here is that this was based on the increased expense for the ongoing phase 3 clinical study it is working on, and it needs to review this expense amount to make sure it is correct.

With that being said, Greenwich did provide an update on cash burn in January of 2026. It mentioned that it doubled its cash balance for the close of January 23, 2026 to be $12.5 million. This was thanks to having raised $7 million in the first three weeks of January 2026 utilizing its ATM agreement in place. It's important to highlight that this is given as preliminary unaudited financials; the final numbers may differ. It mentions that it is expected to see an increase of expenses in 2026/2027. The risk here is that it is going to have to keep tapping into this ATM agreement to generate cash for its operations. The only way to avoid such fundraising through this ATM is to develop a collaboration agreement of some sort. Otherwise, it is just going to have to keep utilizing this financial instrument going forward.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Greenwich LifeSciences. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study using GLSI-100 for the treatment of HER2-positive patients who are at high risk for disease recurrence and have completed both neoadjuvant and postoperative adjuvant standard of care therapy. A conservative number of a reduced risk disease recurrence rate of 70% has been set by the company to be met by this ongoing FLAMINGO-01 study. The risk here is that even though it was able to achieve an 80% reduction of disease recurrence in the phase 2b study, there is no guarantee that this will be replicated in the ongoing phase 3 study. Secondly, the company has the flexibility to seek approval for going after both HLA-A*02 and non-HLA-A*02 HER2 positive BC patients. There is no guarantee that that therapy will be shown to work for one or both of these specific HLA patient populations.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the patent claims the company just mentioned. This was based on statistically significant open label immune response and recurrence rate data. The goal is to seek patent claims for non-HLA-A*02 patients based on this data and achieve patent protection through 2045. The risk here is that there is no assurance that such a patent will be provided to this company, which could really be a bad outcome for it. Speaking of the open-label immune response and recurrence data, this is another risk to consider relating to the patent claims. Why is that? That's because this was taken from the FLAMINGO-01 study, which remains blinded at the present time. There are several factors here to be aware of. First and foremost, the data shown is based on the first patients recruited thus far. Secondly, these open-label results were given from clinical sites and have not been completely reviewed. Lastly, there is no assurance that the results shown thus far in the open-label portion of this study are going to be equivalent to the final outcome data to be released later on.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. The reason why is because the company continues to believe that its expenses will increase in 2026/2027 first and foremost. Plus, in order to counter its yearly cash burn, it has to continue to tap into its ATM. The risk here is that, which is that it continuously needs to raise funds to fund its phase 3 study and operations with an ATM. The risk here is what the company notes, which is that barring the ability to negotiate a licensing/collaboration agreement, it may need to enact a large financing transaction to raise cash. If it takes such a route, it would not only cause the stock price to trade lower, but it could severely dilute shareholders. Another risk to mention relating to its financials would be the late 10-K SEC Filing. It mentioned that it would be able to file this report within the 15-day extension permitted by SEC rules. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this will be filed on time, and the company may need additional time.

Conclusion

Greenwich Life Sciences is continuing to make progress in terms of the development of GLSI-100 to treat HER2-positive BC patients who are at high risk for disease recurrence and have completed both neoadjuvant and postoperative adjuvant standard of care therapy. The reason why is because this trial is still ongoing, and the hope is that it will be able to meet or exceed the expectation of a 70% reduction in disease recurrence rate. I believe that it has done well to continue this phase 3 program, plus the two key developments it made since the last time I wrote about this biotech.

The first of which is the open-label data from the phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study, which showed that non-HLA-A*02 HER2-positive BC patients achieved an 80% reduction in recurrence rate. However, it is important to mention that this was based on preliminary data from an ongoing blinded FLAMINGO-01 study. The beauty of this company is the change of protocol, for which, in the United States, it started to recruit both HLA-A*02 and non-HLA-A*02 HER2-positive BC patients. This means that its drug could be equipped to help reduce disease recurrence rate in these patients regardless of HLA status. With the phase 3 FLAMINGO-01 study still ongoing for the time being, plus the two key developments mentioned above, I believe that Greenwich LifeSciences should be kept at a "Buy" rating.

data.title: XRPI: XRP Exposure With Yield, But Structural Disadvantages Remain
data.description:
In a previous article, I recently covered the Volatility Shares Solana ETF (SOLZ). In that article, I argued that due to SOLZ’s higher expense ratio, lack of access to staking yield, and structural inefficiencies, investors would fare better investing in Solana spot ETFs.

That said, I now turn to another Volatility Shares ETF, the XRP ETF (XRPI). While structurally similar to other futures-based crypto funds (i.e., no direct token holdings and instead, exposure via derivatives), I argue that XRPI fills a gap in the market that the other Volatility Shares futures-based fund (SOLZ) does not achieve.

Despite a considerably higher expense ratio (when compared to spot ETFs) and tracking inefficiencies, the yield component is attractive for investors looking for both XRP exposure and income. For these reasons, I assign a ‘Hold’ rating to XRPI.

Fund Overview

The volatility Shares XRP ETF (XRPI) began trading on May 22nd, 2025, before the approval of XRP spot-based products. The fund issuer has a number of crypto-based futures ETFs providing both 1x and 2x coverage to tokens such as Bitcoin, Ethereum, XRP, and Chainlink.

The fund’s objective is long-term capital appreciation, mirroring the returns of XRP-USD.

According to the fund’s prospectus,

“[XRPI] seeks to achieve its investment objective primarily through managed exposure to XRP futures contracts that trade only on an exchange registered with the CFTC (“XRP Futures Contracts”), and cash, cash-like instruments or high quality securities that serve as collateral to the Fund’s investments in XRP Futures Contracts (“Collateral Investments”). In seeking its investment objective, the Fund seeks to participate in 100% of the returns of XRP.”

With that said, the fund does not maintain holdings in the XRP token itself, but rather derivatives such as futures and swaps.

Structural Advantages & Disadvantages

Disadvantages

The main disadvantages of the fund are its expense ratio and tracking inefficiencies.

While Volatility Shares is currently waiving a portion of the management fee until June 30th, 2026 (with a current management fee of 0.94%), after the waiver period is over, the management fee will increase to 1.15%. Even with the partial waiver in place, XRPI has a considerably higher fee than all other spot XRP ETFs. Over time, this difference can have a real effect on compounded returns.

Another disadvantage of the fund is the inefficient holding structure.

Like other futures-based ETFs from Volatility Shares, XRPI relies on derivatives rather than directly holding the underlying asset, which can lead to performance deviations over time. In particular, losses can occur from selling lower-priced expiring contracts and buying higher-priced longer-dated ones. Over extended periods, this can have a continued negative effect on returns.

The table below shows 1-month returns, with XRPI underperforming leading XRP ETFs by approximately 115 basis points.

Additionally, in conjunction with the fund’s derivative holdings, a portion of the portfolio must remain in cash and treasuries as collateral. As a result, this limits the full participation in XRP’s upside price appreciation.

When combined with higher fees and price tracking inefficiencies, these structural factors can lead to a return profile that fails to match that of the spot-based ETFs.

Advantages

While I acknowledge the disadvantages, the fund also has several positive factors working in its favor. In particular, AUM and yield are two positive attributes.

Currently, the Canary XRP ETF (XRPC) and the Bitwise XRP ETF (XRP) are the leading spot ETFs based on AUM. Although XRPI falls towards the bottom of the pack of XRP ETFs, the fund still maintains assets under management of over $100 million. This is important, as crypto ETFs are still very new. As such, scale is meaningful. Large AUM translates to tighter spreads, improved institutional adoption, and lower risk of fund closures. While XRPI is not the largest XRP ETF, it maintains a respectable size, larger than smaller funds like GXRP and XRPR.

Another positive attribute is the dividend. Unlike other cryptocurrencies that I have covered (namely, Ethereum and Solana), the XRP network does not offer yield. Therefore, spot XRP ETFs behave more like Bitcoin ETFs, with returns entirely dependent on price appreciation. For this reason, the dividend paid by XRPI offers investors a potential income opportunity (partially offsetting the high fee) while also allowing investors to benefit from the upside potential of the underlying token.

According to Seeking Alpha, XRPI’s current dividend yield (TTM) is 2.98%. That said, the fund has a variable distribution policy that depends predominantly on realized trading gains and income generated from the interest on collateral.

Currently, XRPI pays a monthly, variable distribution rather than fixed dividends, with income primarily generated from XRP futures activity and interest on cash holdings. Due to the fact that payouts depend largely on trading gains and market conditions, these payouts can fluctuate significantly.

Risks to Underlying Thesis

While the yield component may be compelling for some income-oriented investors, general underperformance compared to spot ETFs and the underlying asset suggests further future divergence. In my view, this could have a negative effect on the futures-based fund, as capital flows to more efficient holding spot-based structures. If this were to occur, I would expect to see a continued decline in fund assets.

Additionally, current market conditions remain bearish. Given the risk-off phase I are currently in, it is likely that we continue to see downward pressure on all cryptocurrency ETFs. Given XRPI’s reliance on derivatives rather than holding spot XRP, it is also likely that we could continue to see deviations in performance over time.

Despite the advantages and disadvantages that I have covered in this article, XRPI still serves a potential purpose within the portfolio of certain investors. Namely, for investors who are comfortable with exposure via derivatives, its accessibility via traditional brokerage accounts continues to provide a frictionless way to gain exposure to XRP’s price movements while simultaneously introducing a yield component. While I think that spot-ETFs are generally better for most market participants, futures-based ETFs can still serve a role for individuals seeking exposure without the complexities of direct crypto ownership.

Thesis for Underlying Cryptocurrency

My long-term thesis remains cautiously optimistic for the XRP token.

The XRP Ledger proves to be promising, given its design for payments and cross-border transactions. This is particularly true when considering the use cases for financial institutions being able to transfer value quickly without the need for pre-funded accounts. Its settlement speeds (~3-5 seconds) and low transaction costs make it attractive for high-frequency institutional payment activity, thus supporting my long-term bullish case if adoption continues to grow.

While I have addressed concerns in previous articles, namely stablecoin usage compared to other major blockchains, and a smaller developer base, the continued adoption of the underlying technology (and rollout of new funds like XRPI and spot ETFs) proves to be bullish. With that said, I admit that XRP-USD is perhaps a more specialized network in terms of use cases when compared to other cryptocurrencies such as Ethereum or Solana.

Additionally, I acknowledge that current market conditions appear generally bearish. With major cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin and Ethereum having completed their bull market phases towards the end of 2025, liquidity appears to still be moving along the risk curve out of cryptocurrencies and into lower-risk assets. With XRP-USD currently trading below significant moving averages, it is my view that near-term downward pressure will continue for the immediate foreseeable future.

Final Takeaway

While not without its flaws, XRPI still provides a vehicle for investors to gain exposure to XRP-USD’s potential upside while simultaneously earning yield. The fund’s monthly distribution provides an income component that is otherwise absent in comparable spot XRP ETFs. However, this benefit comes at the added cost of a significantly higher management fee. At present, the distribution more than covers the management fee; however, this might not always be the case.

While the yield component and AUM can currently be seen as a benefit, high fees and structural inefficiencies provide cause for concern. Over time, these factors are likely to result in performance that lags the underlying asset. That said, the ETF may still appeal to investors who prioritize accessibility, income generation, and exposure to derivatives.

Ultimately, while the fund fills a niche within the crypto ETF landscape, I assign a “Hold” rating to XRPI. Additionally, while I remain cautiously optimistic on the long-term prospects of XRP-USD, shorter-term price action is likely to continue facing headwinds

data.title: WAGN: Buffett Style Investing Via This ETF
data.description:
Thesis

The ETF product is very versatile, and while many investors are accustomed to ETFs passively following market indices, the active segment for equity ETFs is also quite large. We have recently stumbled upon such a fund, the Pabrai Wagons ETF (WAGN), which we are going to discuss at length in this article. What makes this ETF interesting is the Buffett style investing mantra it tries to achieve, and its small number of holdings. Its main portfolio manager, Mr. Mohnish Pabrai actually met Mr. Buffett, and is a vocal supporter of the investing style made popular by Buffett.

First Things First — Mutual Fund to ETF Conversion

From a structural standpoint WAGN is a new addition to the investing universe, having converted from a mutual fund to an exchange traded fund in 2026:

The Pabrai Wagons ETF is the successor to the Pabrai Wagons Fund (the “Predecessor Fund”), which operated as a mutual fund. The Predecessor Fund commenced operations on September 29, 2023. The Pabrai Wagons ETF is the successor to the accounting and performance history of the Institutional Share Class of the Predecessor Fund. Any historical information provided for the Pabrai Wagons ETF that relates to the periods prior to February 6, 2026, is that of the Predecessor Fund. The Pabrai Wagons ETF began trading on NYSE Floor February 9, 2026.

It is important to remember this because most data platforms will not show performance metrics prior to 2026, while the fund itself does present figures since inception in 2023. The ETF wrapper ensures daily liquidity, and should help the fund grow its AUM versus the mutual fund structuring.

Some of the current ETF analytics are:

  • AUM: $153 million
  • Expense ratio: 0.9%
  • CUSIP: 74316P538
  • Ticker/Exchange: WAGN / NYSE

What Does the Fund Actually Do?

Let us look at the mission statement posted by the name:

We believe exceptional investment performance is usually the product of only a few great investments held for a very long time; the trick is to not only find the great investments, but also not sell them. Reminiscent of the defensive maneuver of American pioneers in the 1800s, the Fund will “circle the wagons” around its highest conviction holdings, which it believes will have the highest potential for investment returns. If the Fund needs to sell securities to meet cash needs, it will generally avoid selling these high conviction securities first.

The fund is an actively managed ETF which invests in global equities. There are no constraints here, thus the mandate is very wide. In fact, investors in the fund are underwriting Mohnish Pabrai's investment acumen. Since WAGN is not a passive fund following an index, nor an active ETF focusing on a particular market sector or geography, investors in the name are simply putting their faith in Mr. Pabrai.

For names like WAGN, which are basically like a hedge-fund, it is important to see how the fund does historically and what type of analytics it produces: that is drawdowns, annual growth rates and net NAV moves, corroborated with risk/reward ratios and beta to the market. Since the fund is new, we do not have any of that, although we can see limited information since the fund was started as a mutual fund.

Current fund composition

The ETF is true to its ethos, with a very limited portfolio:

When excluding cash and currency hedges, the name only contains 20 equities, some which have a high concentration. Warrior Met Coal (HCC) for example makes up 11% of the portfolio. As per Seeking Alpha, HCC:

Warrior Met Coal, Inc. engages in the production and export of non-thermal steelmaking coal for the steel production by metal manufacturers in Europe, South America, and Asia. It offers hard-coking coal through the operation of underground mines located in Alabama. The company also sells natural gas extracted as a byproduct from coal production. Warrior Met Coal, Inc. was incorporated in 2015 and is headquartered in Brookwood, Alabama.

HCC has a 'Buy' rating by Seeking Alpha analysts, and a 'Hold' rating assigned by the quant system.

The second highest holding in the portfolio is Edelweiss Financial Services Ltd (EDEL.IN), a company listed in India which is an investment and financial services enterprise:

As per its listing, the market value for EDEL is roughly $1 billion. The ETF also has a hedge in place for the FX component for EDEL.

Another large holding in the ETF which represents an international company is TAV Havalimanlari Holding AS (TAVHY). TAV is also known as TAV Airports, and represents a leading global airport operator based in Turkey. The company operates 15 airports in eight countries—primarily across Turkey, Georgia, Kazakhstan, Tunisia, and Saudi Arabia. The name is listed on the Istanbul Stock exchange, and has a market value of roughly $2.7 billion. Again, the ETF has a foreign exchange hedge in place for this investment.

The fund does have concentration limitations in place, which they detail as following:

* Not more than 25% of the total assets of the Pabrai Wagons ETF (the "Fund") will be invested in the securities of a single issuer.

* Not more than 25% of the Fund's total assets will be invested in any one industry or groups of industries.

* With respect to 50% of the Fund’s total assets, not more than 5% of the Fund’s total assets will be invested in the securities of a single issuer and the Fund will not own more than 10% of the outstanding voting securities of a single issuer.

We can see from the composition that the ETF can and does make international bets, and while the fund currently hedges a significant portion of its foreign exchange exposure, it does not always have to do so.

Performance

As we have stated before, funds like WAGN live and die by their performance metrics. Since the name does not follow a specific market segment or index, it represents a total return play. This simply means investors need to make money out of holing WAGN, otherwise there is no investment thesis to be had (i.e. hold some S&P 500 exposure, etc). While financial platforms do not provide data prior to 2026, the fund does show metrics since it was incorporated as a mutual fund:

The net return metrics are impressive, especially when looking at the performance year to date. However, the story is not complete without having tangible risk metrics, such as volatility, standard deviation and drawdowns. Just by looking at its components we see a very volatile set of holdings, thus we are very much keen to see how the fund does from an analytics standpoint now that it is listed as an ETF. For example, WAGN states it produced a 15% annualized NAV return since inception, but the question is regarding the volatility. Did it make that return with a volatility profile superior to the S&P 500 or a more volatile one?

We like the WAGN ethos and the concept of picking a few good names and riding them, but the name lives and dies by its performance metrics. In order to make an informed decision an investor needs to follow closely this ETF in the next year to gauge its risk profile and market performance. We find WAGN to be promising and worth keeping a tab on.

Factors to watch

For this fairly new fund there are a number of factors to watch in order to get a better sense of the alignment between the fund management talk and the actual performance:

1. Portfolio Turnover

One of the defining factors for WAGN is its propensity to pick good names and stick with them. If the fund ends up having a high turnover in the next year, it should tell investors that the manager is not really doing what it preaches.

2. Portfolio Volatility

Achieving high returns with a massive volatility is not the same story or pitch as achieving high returns with a low volatility profile. Imagine you have a stock portfolio that ends up at +12% for the year but had -40% drawdowns. Now compare that with a portfolio that ends up at +12% but only had a -7% drawdown. Which one would be more appealing? We are keen to see what type of volatility WAGN posts.

3. FX Hedging

One of the stories of 2025 was around a weak dollar and the impact to international equities. WAGN has chosen to hedge its exposures so far, and to note that the Turkish lira has been on a down-path in the past few years, with the central bank recently selling gold to defend the currency. Not all currencies are created equal, and we are keen to see if WAGN continues fully hedging out this risk factor.

Conclusion

WAGN is an equities ETF. The name is a new addition, having converted from a mutual fund in early 2026. The ETF invests in global equities, and has a wide mandate. The fund only contains 20 names currently, and the main portfolio manager has a philosophy trying to emulate Buffett via a buy and hold strategy for good names. The ETF presents a limited operating history, having started as a mutual fund in 2023. Since inception the name has posted robust returns, but we would need more historical analytics for the fund. We are adding this name to our watchlist.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Apple's Cheap AI Bet Is Paying Off Big
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It would surprise many, but Apple Inc. (AAPL) has actually been exceedingly good at delivering the promise of AI.

The iPhone maker is not delivering AI in the typical fashion that the world has become accustomed to—by releasing frontier models like OpenAI’s (OPENAI) GPT. Instead, Apple quietly chugged along the sidelines, keeping its head down and doubling down on its strategy to launch great hardware, tie it back to its sticky ecosystem, and engineer privacy. Apple used AI to quietly tighten up its ecosystem further while employing a hybrid approach to resurrect Siri’s AI version.

So far this plan seems to be working because Apple's iPhone sales are surging ahead while management delivers to markets the one metric that matters most when it comes to AI in 2026—ROI. Rumors like the reports today about the company apparently falling behind on the production schedule of its foldable iPhones are hitting the stock.

But I argue why it's important to trust Apple’s management with their hardware plans and that Apple is in a unique position to be a winner in 2026, making me bullish on its shares.

Why Apple’s AI Strategy is Unique - and Rewarding

Apple has been the subject of intense speculation with regard to its transformative stance in AI, especially after it had started to trip over its Siri upgrade roadmap last year.

As recently as today, Nikkei Asia reported that the iPhone company

“is encountering setbacks in the engineering test phase of its first-ever foldable iPhone, which could lead to delays in its mass production and product shipment schedule.”

This news was refuted by Bloomberg in a counter-report a few hours later, which just shows how investors must stay the course and trust Apple’s management, especially when it comes to its hardware device roadmap.

Over the course of last year, Apple’s hardware strategy seemed to be working extremely well, as I had noted in October, leading to Apple notching its best-ever iPhone quarterly sales since 2021, with iPhone sales up 23% last quarter.

The most important thing Apple has done by growing iPhone sales is to widen its distribution network. In the Q1 FY26 call, management reported that its installed base was now at 2.5B, up 8.7%. That's the first time in 4 years that Apple grew its installed base.

Remember, the surging iPhone sales and accelerating installed base come at a time when the broader perspective was that Apple “fell behind in the AI race.” Mainly because Apple was perceived to be defaulting on its big Siri AI upgrade. That was the story in 2025.

In 2026, everything has changed because markets are even more laser-focused on ROI. Gross margins, free cash margins, and capital efficiency metrics are the order of the day. Apple’s management has been expanding its R&D budgets at a minimal growth pace while delivering record-high gross margins of 47%. And management projects these gross margins to get even better, “between 48% and 49%," as noted on the Q1 FY26 call. Most importantly, Apple’s capex budgets are incredibly cheap, with the company spending just ~3% of its revenues on capex. None of the other Magnificent 7 companies are being as frugal, with almost all Mag 7 companies spending upwards of 20% of their revenues on capex.

In the last year, Apple has reportedly moved towards its baseline strategy, i.e., “recommitting to its core business model: selling hardware and services.” That involves its popular iPhone 17 lineup and carving out a niche but powerful product category for itself in the higher education market with the MacBook Neo. At the same time, Apple is redirecting its resources for building AI features in the App Store and engineering search-like feature strategies in its operating system platforms. Such AI features are not front-facing and fancy like the tangible agentic features, which can be seen today, but work in the background to tighten up Apple’s ecosystem even further to provide users with a seamless experience. When it comes to Siri, Apple is reportedly moving towards deploying a dual approach with infusing AI into Siri. Remember, Apple is working on this product strategy with AI on a frugal budget, which involves spending just 8.5% of its revenues on R&D and ~3% of its revenues on capex.

Perhaps the most telling takeaway of Apple’s product strategy is the ROI of its investments. On a TTM basis, there are only 2 out of all the Mag7 companies that are accelerating their ROIC. Apple happens to be one of them, reporting a 68% ROIC, while Nvidia (NVDA) happens to be the other. Despite spending far less than its Mag-7 peers, Apple is already demonstrating a high payoff on its frugal capex spend.

The returns that Apple is generating on its invested capital, as seen in Exhibit D, remind me of this one line the WSJ recently wrote on a similar viewpoint about Apple:

Has Apple fumbled the AI revolution? Not at all. It is making a cold-eyed bet that the most frenzied build-out in the history of American capitalism will produce inadequate returns.

So, yes, Apple may have been cheap so far, but it’s probably going to have the last laugh, where the company can demonstrate that its wide distribution of its installed base will respond to its AI strategy that is tightly integrated across Apple’s ecosystem. This leaves Apple as the gatekeeper, collecting rent from its customers and its AI vendors, keeping investors happy with the returns on their investments. That means Apple eventually has happy customers, happy vendors, and happy investors.

Apple’s Valuations Are Rich for a Reason

I have noted a few times in the past why Apple’s valuations look rich, but that might be warranted.

Apple has delivered four types of accelerations this year, all at once, which is what the market is ready to pay up for—top-line growth, gross profits, EPS, and ROIC. While top-line growth is expected to accelerate by 12%, EPS growth is expected to accelerate by 14% this year. Markets are currently valuing Apple at ~29-30x forward EPS.

I strongly suspect markets will have a clearer path to valuing Apple closer to 34-35x forward earnings as we get closer to Apple’s WWDC '26 event in June. That calls for ~mid-teens upside in Apple’s shares.

Risks to My Thesis

Apart from the Iran war, which could be a strong headwind to the world’s supply chain, management has suggested that memory costs will likely rise this year. Apple is redirecting its capital resources towards fortifying its supply chain resilience, and memory is a big part of that. Management has said that despite rising memory costs, the company will be able to deliver gross margin expansion, which is incredible for the company.

Other sources are indicating that Apple is investing its cash in an aggressive way to give it a competitive advantage in the supply chain when it comes to memory. This could be risky, but given Apple’s CEO’s background in operations and supply chain management, I believe the company might be doing the right thing to prioritize supply chain resilience over AI capex.

Takeaway

In summary, I believe Apple’s prudent and cautious approach to AI will continue to prevail, leaving the iPhone maker in a strong position to demonstrate sustained growth and deliver margin expansion and ROI at the same time.

I am bullish on Apple’s shares heading into its WWDC event in June.

data.title: Bank of America Earnings: A Pivotal Report For 2026 Expectations
data.description:
Bank of America (BAC) is scheduled to report its Q1 2026 results next week, and the report will be loaded with high expectations as investors will be looking for clues that the banking sector could withstand the ongoing market uncertainty.

Just a couple of months ago, in the early days of 2026, the banking sector appeared unstoppable, with the State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) up nearly 17% on a 12-month basis. Bank of America was among the best large-cap performers in the space with a total return of almost 24%.

In early January I also warned investors against certain risks that were lurking beneath the surface and retained my hold rating on BAC. Since then, things have turned for the worse, with both BAC and XLF falling by more than 8% over the course of 3 months.

As expected, BAC's elevated price/book multiple has come down from its multi-year highs, and this could now be reason enough for some investors to turn bullish on the stock. The problem is that the upcoming quarter could turn sour for investors, as some macro risks could weigh on not only the quarterly results but the rest of the fiscal year as well.

A Changing Landscape

BofA's share price performance in 2025 was largely driven by the ongoing bull market in equities and the strong growth of the bank's net interest income (NII).

The annual growth rate of NII from 2015 to 2024 was 4%, and it accelerated to 7% in FY 2025 and 10% in the last quarter of the year.

When the company reported its Q4 2025 results, however, the guidance for 2026 was for NII to grow within the 5% to 7% range. Although this might seem like a slowdown from 2025, it is still above the historical average. The management, however, highlighted the fact that this forecast was based on the yield curve and the anticipation of two rate cuts in 2026.

With regard to a forward view of NII, let me give you a few thoughts. At Investor Day in November, we indicated our expectation that we would see 5% to 7% growth in net interest income in 2026 compared to 2025, and that's still our belief today based on the latest interest rate curve, which includes two rate cuts in 2026.

Source: BofA Q4 2025 Earnings Transcript

This assumption no longer holds as the market has reassessed its future expectations when it comes to the short-end of the curve. At the moment, the highest implied probability is for no rate cuts or hikes through late 2027.

As a result, the yield on 2-year Treasuries has skyrocketed from around 3.5% at the beginning of the year to almost 3.9% as of today.

The long-end of the curve did not experience such sharp movement, and the steepening of the yield curve has now reversed.

This is not good news for BofA's profitability in 2026, as the slope of the yield curve affects overall return on capital.

That is why even BofA's short-term guidance for the second quarter of this year could have profound implications for shareholder returns in the case of it being below current expectations.

The State Of The U.S. Consumer

The health of the U.S. consumer will be another focal point during the upcoming earnings release.

In 2025, loan growth came primarily from the bank's commercial portfolio, while consumer loans were already showing some signs of weakness with a 4% year-on-year growth during the last quarter of the year.

Loans in Q4 of $1.17 trillion improved $90 billion or 8% year-over-year, driven by 12% commercial loan growth. Consumer loans grew at a slower 4% year-over-year pace and importantly were up across every loan category of card, mortgage, auto and home equity.

Source: BofA Q4 2025 Earnings Transcript

The 12% year-on-year growth in commercial loans was impressive and did the heavy-lifting when it came to profitability improvement for the bank. However, it is worth noting that a large proportion of this growth was realized in the first half of the year, with quarterly growth slowing down in Q4.

Since BofA's last earnings report, consumer confidence has continued to decline, and the index now stands at record lows. According to the latest report, it ticked up in March, but remains at dangerously low levels.

We should keep in mind that none of that takes into account the impact that recent geopolitical events in the Middle East would have on consumer spending. Gasoline prices across the U.S. have skyrocketed in recent weeks, and this is now a major threat for the overall health of U.S. consumers.

Recent events at Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) have also sent some early signs of trouble within the private credit market. So far this does not have any direct implications for large financial institutions like BAC, but it increases the probability of credit tightening over the coming months.

Overall, BAC's Q1 results would be very important when it comes to either confirming or refuting some of investors' fears about the state of the U.S. consumer. Right now, it appears highly likely that recent events could derail the fragile growth in the consumer loan portfolio for the bank.

Conclusion

Bank of America's upcoming Q1 report will be of critical importance when it comes to evaluating the impact of recent macroeconomic and geopolitical events on the bank's bottom line. On one hand, we have the shift in interest rate expectations, which could provide a headwind for profitability. On the other, there are risks for consumer and commercial lending, which could result in a major slowdown of loan growth.

data.title: Aptera Motors: Just Keep Selling
data.description:
Last week, we received fourth-quarter results from Aptera Motors (SEV) for its December 2025 fiscal period. The solar-powered electric vehicle maker is getting closer to the market launch of its first product, but the niche company remains in a tough financial spot. With shares having more than doubled from their public trading low, investors are likely being handed another chance to sell.

Previous coverage of the name

The last time I took a look at Aptera was back in November, right after the company had announced its third-quarter results. While I noted that the quarterly loss had come down from the prior year period, the company didn't have much cash, and further equity raises were likely. Since that time, Aptera shares have plunged by roughly 62%, compared to a less than 3% decline in the S&P 500.

A look at Q4 results

Aptera remains at least a few quarters away from generating revenues. The company reported a GAAP net loss of $15.48 million in Q4 2025, up from $8.88 million a year earlier. The main reason was a more than 50% surge in operating expenses, as the company looks to begin production of vehicles for sale. On a non-GAAP basis, Aptera reported a smaller $8.71 million loss in Q4, but this was also up quite a bit from the $5.32 million seen in Q4 2024.

Back in early March, the company announced that it had completed the first vehicle off its validation assembly line, a major operational milestone. While the initial goal of ramping production to 20,000 vehicles in 2026 has been delayed due to funding issues, management stated in its 10-K filing that it still aims to deliver its first customer vehicles this year.

As we get closer to actual production, we hopefully will have more street analysts covering the stock. Currently, just one analyst has any full-year revenue estimates on Aptera, calling for only a couple hundred thousand dollars this year. That number soars to $37.5 million next year and further to $130 million in 2028, but early-stage electric vehicle names have certainly been known for disappointments on the growth front so far this decade.

Balance sheet and cash flow update

Aptera finished 2025 with $9.6 million in cash and equivalents, down from about $12 million at the end of Q3. For the full year, cash burn came in at nearly $16.7 million, although this was an improvement from the more than $26 million burned in 2024. With losses ongoing and the need to continue building production facilities, cash burn is expected to continue for the indefinite future.

During Q1, Aptera had two major capital-raising events. The first was a follow on equity offering, which brought in $8.2 million after expenses. Another $8.1 million in gross proceeds was raised through warrant exercises, so we should see a nice boost in the cash total when we get the Q1 2026 financials. The downside is that investors have been diluted a bit, and at fairly low prices compared to where the stock was when it went public.

Nearly 36.6 million shares of common stock were outstanding as of March 20th, up from less than 28 million at the end of last year. It would not surprise me to see that share count continue to soar moving forward, between both share-based compensation expenses and additional capital raises. As a reminder, the company had stated that it would require $65 million in total capital to reach low-volume production and another $140 million to $160 million for the second ramp to get to high volume.

The current valuation picture

As of noon on Wednesday, Aptera was trading at $2.70 a share, giving the company a market cap of just under $99 million. Obviously there are no positive earnings here, and there aren't even any revenues yet, so traditional valuation metrics don't really apply just yet. If we look out to 2027, however, the stock trades at 2.64 times that one analyst's revenue estimate.

Except for Tesla (TSLA), every other electric vehicle name I cover trades at less than 1.75 times next year's expected revenue, and all of those names have annual sales in the billions of dollars. There is just one analyst rating on the stock currently, a strong buy with a $10 price target, which represents massive upside from current levels.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Aptera reported its Q4 results recently, showing increased losses as the company hopefully gets to its first vehicle deliveries this year. With cash burn remaining the biggest issue currently, management was able to raise some decent capital over the first few months of this year, but it did dilute investors quite handily. To get to true volume production, a lot more cash will need to be raised, and with a valuation near $100 million currently, that likely will be quite painful for current holders.

I am maintaining my sell rating on the stock here today. Aptera is still at least a few quarters away from generating any revenue, and it remains to be seen if the company's vehicle will find substantial consumer interest in a very competitive automotive industry. With the stock having more than doubled from its low and the need for continued significant capital infusions, I just don't see shares holding up very well this year.

data.title: Iran Could Be The Tipping Point For The Consumer-Led Economy
data.description:
All eyes this week will be on the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and its continued impact on energy and commodity prices across the globe.

The second key focal point for investors will be around the private credit markets, where new cockroaches have consistently emerged since issues first became apparent at Blue Owl Capital (OWL) in the back half of February. These worries increased markedly in March as myriad large private credit funds 'gated' increasing redemption requests from their investors.

However, prudent investors should not take their eyes off the increasing struggles of most of the consumer population. After all, consumer activity drives roughly two-thirds of economic activity in the U.S. It is important to note that most consumers were already struggling coming into 2026.

With recent revisions, job growth only averaged 15,000 jobs monthly in 2025. The headline March BLS jobs report was much stronger than expected, after the February BLS jobs report showed 92,000 positions were lost. In addition, the March jobs numbers were not as strong as the headlines, as I articulated in this article. And as I noted in another recent piece, AI-related (for many reasons) job losses are just starting.

Widespread consumer stress can be seen in the increasing delinquency rates across the roughly $4.5 trillion in combined student, auto, and credit card debt (above). Let's get into the weeds on some of the concerning data points around key consumer loan balances.

After four years of taxpayer-financed largesse, payments and collection processes were restarted on student loans in 2025. The impact was pretty immediate. As of the end of 2025, some 7.7 million of the just over 40 million individuals with student loans were in default. That represents roughly 11% of student loan balances, or some $180 billion in default. Another 4.4 million student loans were 30 days past due.

This obviously negatively impacts the buyer pool for homes and other large-ticket purchases that typically get financed, like for vehicles. Speaking of which, roughly three million vehicles were repossessed in 2025. The highest level since 2009 during the Great Financial Crisis.

The top 10% of American households by income continue to hold up well. This cohort now represents roughly 50% of all consumer spending. This is up significantly from the mid-30s a couple of generations ago. Given this demographic's total holdings of stock market and other wealth, as long as equities don't go into a bear market, real estate values don't fall significantly, and/or AI-related job losses don't start to hit the high-end professional class, this part of the consumer population should continue to be a key economic engine going forward.

However, what is happening to the majority of the rest of the U.S. consumer base is increasingly concerning. Let's take a look at the subprime market. These are individuals with a credit score of 660 or below across the personal loan space. The subprime delinquency rate has more than tripled since the Covid forbearance lows and has moved sharply up to 10%. This is a level not seen since 2014, when the U.S. was finally fully emerging from the Great Financial Crisis.

Subprime credit represents 15% of all household debt outstanding (which includes mortgages), or $2.7 trillion. It should be noted that subprime credit was roughly $3.5 trillion in 2008, when subprime delinquency rates hit 19% at their peak. We are not near there yet, but the trend in delinquency rates is troubling.

The conflict in the Middle East has just exacerbated this stress. Gas prices have shot up by roughly a third to north of four bucks a gallon for the first time since 2022. Diesel prices have moved from a national average of $3.50 a gallon to nearly $5.60 a gallon. And given that almost every good on the market is transported via rail or truck, logistical costs will be very negatively impacted. The United States Postal Service and Amazon (AMZN) are just two major names that have added 'temporary' surcharges to cover these additional fuel costs.

Jet fuel costs have roughly doubled since late February, when the first bombs started dropping in Iran. United Airlines (UAL) and other airlines have already hiked checked-in baggage fees to offset these costs. Jet fuel is approximately 20% of airlines' operational costs, so consumers can expect fares and fees at airlines to move up in the weeks ahead.

A large percentage of the globe's LNG, nitrogen, phosphate, and urea travels through the Strait of Hormuz. These are key inputs for the production of fertilizer, whose price has surged since this regional war commenced. This is a key cost of agricultural production, and this will boost food inflation in the months and quarters ahead with a lag.

Last week, the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow reduced its estimate for first-quarter GDP growth again from 1.9% to 1.6%, roughly half the projection prior to the outbreak in hostilities. The war in Iran only occurred during the last month of the quarter, it should be noted. Greater impacts will occur in the second quarter unless this conflict sees a speedy resolution. A scenario that feels unlikely at the moment.

This war will continue to dominate the financial headlines. Private credit will also get its share of focus with Jamie Dimon out with his latest warning around this space on Monday. However, prudent investors should not take their eyes off the increasing stress on much of the consumer population, given consumers are the key economic engine for the U.S. economy. This is especially true as equity valuations remain historically elevated even with the pullback in the indexes so far in 2026.

data.title: Cipher Digital: From Bitcoin Miner To AI Landlord
data.description:
Until recently, Cipher Digital Inc. (CIFR) was a second-tier, pure-play Bitcoin miner created via SPAC in 2021 and led by CEO Tyler Page, a former Goldman Sachs banker. Today, it is a totally different company. CIFR’s business model is transitioning into a data center developer for hyperscalers; essentially, it is swapping a highly volatile cash flow stream linked to crypto for stable, long-term rents secured at attractive prices. Analysts’ consensus forecasts revenue of $235 million for FY2026, but this is just a fraction of what lies ahead given the rent agreements already signed plus a robust pipeline of new projects. My thesis is that due to the business model transition and the consequent ‘dry spell’ in results until H2 FY2026, when the first rents kick in, the market is not yet fully pricing in the shift in the company’s core nature. I rate the stock as a BUY.

The Cinderella Story

As a reader, I have always been drawn to fairy tales, which is perhaps why the Cinderella story was the first thing that came to mind when I started researching this stock. During the crypto winter, Cipher was the humble ‘little sister’ mining Bitcoin in a corner of Texas, focusing on rock-bottom energy costs, while all the attention went to its ‘ugly stepsisters,’ large-scale miners like MARA Holdings (MARA) and Riot Platforms (RIOT). Following the analogy, the fairy godmother did not arrive with a wand but with an Nvidia (NVDA) chip. Cipher’s management realized that its infrastructure—liquid-cooled data centers connected to low-cost energy in Texas- could be used for more than just mining crypto; it could be transformed into a ‘golden carriage’ for AI hyperscale data centers. Last February, the company rebranded from Cipher Mining (the miner’s rags) to Cipher Digital, the ball gown it now wears to present itself to the AI infrastructure market, reflecting a complete business model transformation. From Bitcoin miner to AI landlord.

The firm is entering the HPC data center industry (the ballroom) at the perfect moment. The global market for AI data centers is expected to grow from $344 billion in 2025 to over $2 trillion in 2032 (a 27.5% CAGR), and McKinsey estimates that the installed capacity in the US will grow to 90+ GW by 2030 from approximately 30 GW in 2025. The bottleneck currently is not capital, as hyperscalers and cloud providers are deploying hundreds of billions in capex annually, but rather access to land with guaranteed energy. In West Texas, for example, extending a high-capacity transmission line is a very rigorous and bureaucratic process that can take a few years. Cipher possesses the blessing of being in the right place at the right time, as it essentially controls strategic assets.

Existing Assets

Cipher currently has three energized sites with a total gross capacity of 807 MW, of which 600 MW are allocated to HPC, while the remaining 207 MW stay in Bitcoin mining. In addition, the company has already secured three long-term leases with investment-grade tenants.

Black Pearl (Texas—300 MW). In November 2025, Cipher signed a 15-year lease with AWS with a $5.5B TCV and a rent commencement date in October 2026. Amazon (AMZN) provides a full corporate guarantee on all base rent and operating expenses, resulting in a project-level NOI margin of nearly 100%. The contract includes three five-year renewal options, providing revenue visibility for up to 30 years. The site is already transitioning from crypto mining to HPC; Bitcoin rigs have been redeployed to other locations or put up for sale, as approximately 85% of the existing infrastructure is being repurposed for the data center. With an 85% loan-to-cost ratio, the project is financed via $2.0B in 6.125% senior secured bonds. These are non-recourse to the holding company and will be amortized directly by the lease cash flows.

Barber Lake (Texas—300 MW). This is another energized location where the company signed a 10-year lease with Fluidstack and Google (GOOGL), featuring two five-year extension options, a $3.8B TCV, and an 86% NOI margin. Google is providing a financial backstop for up to $1.73B of Fluidstack’s obligations—effectively an investment-grade guarantee. Management has secured a $1.73B non-recourse bond at a 7.125% coupon to fund the project, keeping the loan-to-cost ratio at 71%. I find it encouraging that the lease’s own cash flows will handle the amortization. Everything seems to be clicking operationally, and that October 2026 target for rent commencement holds steady. To date, there are no reported overruns. The fact that the entire workforce and 95% of long-lead equipment are already secured suggests that the execution risk is actually quite low.

As the chart above shows, these two contracts combined are set to generate an average annual NOI of $669 million over the base lease term, hitting $754 million by 2035. With the current $5.2 billion market cap, the market is paying roughly 7.8x average NOI—signaling that the stock could be undervalued, especially since we haven't even factored in the pipeline yet. Plus, on March 25, 2026, Cipher announced a third 15-year deal with an investment-grade hyperscaler (whose identity remains under wraps). This contract will be developed on one of the company’s existing non-energized sites, underscoring that management is actively working through the pipeline.

Odessa (207 MW—residual crypto mining). Odessa remains the only asset still operating under the mining model, backed by a fixed-price PPA through July 2027. Management is currently weighing whether to convert the site to HPC or sell it once the PPA expires. Regardless, it serves as a short-term liquidity source to fund the transition, and the physical asset itself—207 MW of capacity in West Texas—holds significant market value independent of its eventual use.

The Pipeline

To recap, Cipher currently has 807 MW of energized capacity. Of this total, 207 MW is dedicated to Bitcoin mining, while the remaining 600 MW is split between two sites: 300 MW under contract with Fluidstack/Google and another 300 MW with AWS. This is quite attractive on its own—especially with the stock trading at just 7.8x the combined data center NOI—but in my view, Cipher’s real value lies in the scale of its 3.4 GW pipeline.

The company’s prospective developments can be broken down into three layers. The first layer consists of 370 MW across three sites with lower expected capacity but shorter lead times, as all are expected to come online before 2028. Stingray (100 MW) has its interconnect approved and the substation under construction, targeting Q4 2026 for energization. Reveille, for example, has secured its interconnection approval, and the substation work is underway. Lastly, Ulysses (200 MW) is the first site outside of Texas, recently added with an approved PJM network interconnect and a Q4 2027 target.

The second layer features large-scale sites in West Texas expected to energize in 2028. McLennan, a 500 MW site, has approved ERCOT studies, while Colchis (1,000 MW) and Mikeska (500 MW) have their studies currently in progress awaiting approval. Finally, the third layer is comprised of two 500 MW projects, the Barber Lake extension and Milsing, which is particularly attractive given its proximity to Houston.

Looking at this description, the pipeline looks attractive in GW terms, but that is only one side of the coin; the other is pricing leverage. With hyperscalers eager for power-ready land, Cipher is sitting in the driver’s seat at the negotiating table. This supply-demand gap is a massive tailwind for future margins, as noted by management during the most recent conference call.

As lease pricing continues to move in our favor alongside growing demand, we expect lease economics here to be among the most favorable we've achieved to date.

Source: Q4 2025 Earnings Call

For investors, this suggests that the next phase of the pipeline might be even more lucrative than the contracts already in place.

The Stock Looks Undervalued

Valuing a company like Cipher is a difficult task because, as it pivots its business model, historical financials offer little insight into what the business will generate in the future. Since looking at the rearview mirror provides no clues, one might think looking at comparables is the solution, but the reality is this isn't the case either—as it belongs to a new industry where participants have varying business models and maturity levels, with many players who, like Cipher, aren't even generating revenue yet. In this context, determining a fair value for the shares would be an illusion.

But all is not lost. Sometimes, knowing what something isn't worth can be just as useful for the decision process. To that end, I’m proposing a 'floor' valuation based on the two contracts in place, relying on available data and conservative assumptions. Once we’ve determined the value of these two developments, we can subtract the company’s debt and add cash and Bitcoin holdings to arrive at a NAV per share. At this point—even while deliberately ignoring the entire pipeline—we can calculate the portion of the stock price justified by these 600 MW under contract. This gives us an initial sense of whether the stock is undervalued, but that’s not all.

Given that we have the valuations for Barber Lake and Black Pearl, I’m subtracting project-level debt to arrive at the present value per contracted MW. This figure will then be used to value the pipeline, factoring in the estimated electrification date for each site. At the risk of being accused of heresy by valuation purists, I’m applying a one-third haircut to the pipeline’s MW—assuming that the projects won't be fully electrified or that we might see higher costs at the corporate or project level. Many could argue that this risk is already baked into the cost of capital and is therefore being double-counted, but again, I’m not trying to produce the 'purest' valuation. I’m simply trying to ensure I’m using conservative assumptions to understand if the stock is undervalued.

To determine the cost of capital, I’m using a high debt weighting, following the company's policy of using a large proportion of debt to finance its projects. For the cost of debt, I’ve taken a weighted average of the rates Cipher secured to finance Barber Lake and Black Pearl, and I’m using a 20% cost of equity, which sits more than 1,340 bps above the cost of debt. With a 25% marginal tax rate, the resulting cost of capital is 8.3%. The remaining key assumptions are a 7.5% exit cap rate, 75% NOI renewal factor, and the conversion of half of all potentially dilutive instruments into common stock.

As shown in the model above, I arrive at a $6 billion valuation for the two projects. After adding $0.86 billion in cash and Bitcoin holdings and subtracting CIFR’s $4.77 billion in debt (which includes the notes issued in 2026), we get a NAV per share of $4.2. When compared to the current stock price of $12.82, we can see that one-third of the share price is backed by these 600 MW under contract. Given that the company has a pipeline of an additional 3.4 GW, it’s reasonable to conclude that the stock is undervalued.

As I mentioned earlier, the next step is valuing the pipeline. To achieve this, I’m taking the $6 billion corresponding to the AWS and Fluidstack/Google developments and netting out the $3.73 billion in project-level debt to arrive at a NAV per contracted MW of $3.9 million. By applying this multiple to the pipeline—while applying a 33% haircut across the board except for the recently announced third project—I arrive at a pipeline value of nearly $5.2 billion.

By adding the $5.2 billion to the $2.1 billion NAV before the pipeline, I arrive at a total NAV of $14.5 per share—roughly 13.5% above the current stock price of $12.82. In my view, this valuation is conservative. Not only because of the 33% haircut applied to the pipeline or because I’m assigning zero value to the 207 MW Bitcoin mining operation (which could potentially be converted to HPC), but also because I’m not factoring in any new sites that could be added to the pipeline down the road. To illustrate, if I were to remove the haircut on the non-electrified land, the resulting NAV would be $19.3, representing a 50% upside from current levels. In my opinion, CIFR is clearly undervalued.

Finally, when comparing this valuation to the Wall Street consensus, it’s worth noting that my $14.5 intrinsic value (incorporating the haircut) sits below the minimum price target of $18. Furthermore, the $19.3 NAV per share—without the haircut—remains far below the consensus average target of $27.1.

The Midnight Clock

Returning to the Cinderella analogy, it’s now time to consider the factors that could act as the midnight clock—those events that might break Cipher’s spell.

The most immediate risk is a construction delay at either Black Pearl or Barber Lake. If rents do not begin to flow by October 2026, the anticipated re-rating will be postponed. It’s important to clarify, however, that Cipher does not shoulder the technological risk faced by its clients; its role is to build out the physical infrastructure—land, power, cooling, and fiber—rather than purchasing chips or managing GPU clusters. It is essentially the risk of an industrial real estate developer, which is far more predictable and manageable than pure-play tech risk.

The second risk is dilution. Cipher has warrants issued to Google as part of the Barber Lake deal, plus two series of convertible notes with different conversion prices. Additionally, if credit markets were to tighten, Cipher might need to fund the pipeline through equity. In my view, project-level credit is underwritten against the risk of hyperscalers like Amazon and Google, rather than Cipher’s own balance sheet, which should maintain debt access as long as the tenants' credit quality remains intact.

The third risk is the rising cost of capital. A rate shock could drive up the WACC and compress the contracted NAV. The most powerful mitigation here is demand. AMD CEO Lisa Su was categorical at CES 2026, declaring that we have entered the 'yottascale era.' She estimated the world will need 10,000 times more computing capacity by 2030 than in 2022, emphasizing that AI 'is not hype' but a force changing the lives of society at large. In this context, a sustained decline in HPC infrastructure demand would require an unprecedented dislocation.

The fourth risk is customer concentration, as two tenants account for 100% of current contracts. Diversification arrives with the third lease from March 2026, but the tenant has not been revealed—if their credit quality were inferior to AWS or Google, the market would likely penalize it. Fifth, the electrical grid and regulation. ERCOT batch studies can take several months, and any change in interconnection policy directly impacts the 2028 pipeline schedule. The geographic concentration in Texas also exposes projects to extreme weather events.

Finally, the lack of an HPC operational track record is likely the biggest factor contributing to the valuation discount in my view. Cipher has yet to collect its first check from an HPC lease. In my opinion, Amazon and Google conducted exhaustive due diligence before committing $9.3 billion to an operator without an HPC history.

The Bottom Line

To conclude this article with the analogy, in my view, Cinderella's transformation is ongoing—the gown is on, the carriage is waiting, and hyperscalers have confirmed they will be at the ball. Although I haven't been able to arrive at a precise estimate of Cipher’s intrinsic value, the valuation under what I consider conservative assumptions suggests the stock is currently undervalued. Looking ahead, I anticipate that the commencement of rental income in Q4, significant progress in pipeline execution, or the signing of new contracts with hyperscalers could all act as catalysts for a re-rating. Unlike fairy tales, not every story on Wall Street necessarily has a happy ending; therefore, it is essential to monitor the risks described in the previous section. However, I believe that in Cipher’s case, the risk/reward setup is positive, and for that reason, I am initiating coverage on Cipher with a Buy rating.

data.title: About Those Orders For Durable Goods That Factories In The U.S. Received
data.description:
The 12-month average, which irons out Boeing’s huge orders, shows the trend: +8.8% year-over-year. Orders excluding aircraft & defense jumped by 1.5% in February from January.

When Boeing (BA) receives an order for 30 jet aircraft in one month, that huge order of billions of dollars pushes up durable goods orders that manufacturers in the US receive and that are published monthly. The next month, Boeing might not receive a big order like that, which will push down durable goods orders. We’ll sort that out in a moment.

Total durable goods orders, including aircraft and defense, fell by 1.4% in February from January, to $315 billion, seasonally adjusted. But they were up by 7.3% from a year ago (blue line), according to data from the Census Bureau today.

The 12-month average shows the trend in US manufacturing (red line). It irons out the month-to-month spikes and plunges of huge aircraft orders. In February, it rose by 0.5% from January and by 8.8% year-over-year, not seasonally adjusted. The surge started in early 2025, after flatlining in 2024 and after a multiyear surge out of the pandemic. Orders had essentially stagnated for years before the pandemic.

Orders for nondefense aircraft and parts spike and then plunge by huge amounts, largely driven by orders that Boeing receives. In February, they plunged by 7.7% from January to $19.2 billion. But compared to February last year, orders were still up by 33%.

It’s not that aircraft orders are not important – they’re very important. But they’re randomly volatile on a month-to-month basis in a meaningless way that distorts the month-to-month figures.

In addition, orders for defense aircraft and defense capital goods might be erratic, especially with a war going on, and we can exclude those too to see through the fog.

So, orders excluding aircraft and defense jumped by 1.5% in February from January and by 5.8% year-over-year to a record $278 billion, seasonally adjusted.

The increase of durable goods orders, excluding nondefense aircraft and parts and excluding defense, was driven by month-to-month increases in orders at manufacturers of:

  • Computers & electronic products: +4.9% to $28 billion
  • Motor vehicles & parts: +3.1% to $71 billion
  • Primary metals: +2.2% to $29 billion
  • Machinery: +1.5% to $41 billion
  • Fabricated metal products: +0.5% to $43 billion
  • All other durable goods: +0.5% to $50 billion

Only one major category had even a slight month-to-month dip, electrical equipment, appliances, components: -0.1% to $18 billion. But that was a blip; year-to-date, orders were up by 6.4% from a year ago.

Shifting more manufacturing to the US is crucial for the economy and is crucial for strategic reasons. Trump and Biden were the first presidents in modern times that understood this. Trump 1 encouraged it with tariffs on imported goods to incentivize companies to shift production to the US. Biden encouraged it with large-scale taxpayer-funded grants and loans paid to companies under the CHIPS Act and other programs to build factories in the US. Trump 2 encouraged it by imposing additional wide-ranging tariffs, and he largely maintained the CHIPS Act but rebranded it.

But it’s not instant. Even shifting production from a factory in another country to an existing factory in the US by adding shifts and more automation, such as GM has been doing, takes time. And the process of building a modern factory, from the moment the decision is made until mass production starts, takes several years. Manufacturing is complicated and requires a lot of inputs, but it, and its secondary and tertiary effects, are hugely important to the US economy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
